# Effectiveness of phytoremediation technologies to clean up of metalloids using three plant species in Iran

# Effectiveness of phytoremediation technologies to clean up of metalloids using three plant species in Iran

### Authors

## Abstract

Phytoremediation is a potential, innovative, and cost-effective technology for non-destructive remediation of heavy-metal contaminated soils. A field trial was conducted to evaluate the phytoremediation efficiencies of three plants and the effects of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) or ammonium addition [(NH4)2SO4 and NH4NO3] for assisting removal of heavy metals (Pb, Hg, and Cd) from contaminated soil. The tested plants include Amaranthus retroflexus, Sorghum bicolor, and Lolium perrene. Results showed that maximum concentration of Pb, Hg, and Cd were detected in shoots of A. retroflexus, S. bicolor, and L. perrene at high concentrations in pH=6.2. The application of EDTA as a chelating agent to soil was the most efficient to enhance the phytoavailability of Pb, Hg and Cd. The concentrations of Pb, Hg, and Cd in the shoots of A. retroflexus treated with EDTA were 57 mg/kg, 14.1 mg/kg, and 30 mg/kg, respectively. Results indicated that among the three plants, A. retroflexus had great potential in phytoremediation of contaminated soils.

# Effect of electric field frequency on phytoremediation efficiency and properties of Cd-contaminated soil

[*Environmental Sciences Europe*](/)
**volume 37**, Article number: 126 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

21 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8/metrics)

## Abstract

Electrochemical-phytoextraction is considered as an integrated technology for removal of Cd in contaminated soil. However, there is a lack of systematic studies on the how electric field frequency plant Cd enrichment and soil microbial properties, which has restricted the optimized application of electric field technology. This study investigated the influence of alternating electric current (AC) at frequencies of 50 Hz and 300 Hz on Cd accumulation in willow (*Salix spp*.), soil Cd availability, and soil microbial properties. Exposure to AC at 50 Hz and 300 Hz resulted in different degrees of increase in the biomass production of leaves, branches, stems, and roots; the concentrations of N, P, and K in willow; increased root volume and root surface area; and promoted photosynthesis in willow. Compared with the control, AC at 50 Hz significantly enhanced Cd accumulation in the above-ground parts and roots of willow by 14.65% and 9.48%, respectively. Moreover, exposure to AC significantly increased soil Cd availability by 8.34% and 19.26% at frequencies of 50 Hz and 300 Hz, respectively. AC at 50 Hz and 300 Hz decreased soil urease activity but increased the activity of peroxidase acid, phosphatase, and microbial activity. This suggests that AC electric field modulate the interactions between Cd, enzymes, and microbes. Although Cd generally inhibit enzyme activity, AC electric field can alter the soil environment, reduce toxic effects, and enhance oxidative stress responses and nutrient cycling, which can mitigate Cd toxicity. Therefore, these results indicate that the AC electric field promoted willow growth, increased soil Cd availability, and altered soil properties, ultimately leading to Cd uptake in willow. In particular, 50 Hz AC exhibited greater impacts on plant Cd enrichment.

## Introduction

Cadmium (Cd) contamination severely endangers soil ecosystems and agricultural sustainability. Cd is considered one of the most concerning pollutants due to its high toxicity and bioaccumulate nature. Effective control and remediation of Cd-contaminated soils is therefore critical to safeguarding soil health and food security [[22](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR22 "Hou D, Jia X, Wang L, McGrath S, Zhu Y, Hu Q, Zhao F, Bank M, O’Connor D, Nriagu J (2025) Global soil pollution by toxic metals threatens agriculture and human health. Science. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1126/science.adr5214
                  
                ")]. Phytoremediation has been extensively studied because of its low cost, safety, and reliability [[57](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR57 "Zhu X, Lin F, Sun J, Li X, Zhu G, Lu Y, Sun L, Wang H (2024) Effects of weak electric fields on the denitrification performance of pseudomonas stutzeri: insights into enzymes and metabolic pathways. Microorganisms 12(6):1218. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/microorganisms12061218
                  
                ")]. Phytoremediation of Cd from the contaminated soil generally happens through phytoextraction, phytostabilization, phytodegradation and phytovolatilization processes [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR39 "Muthusaravanan S, Sivarajasekar N, Vivek J, Paramasivan T, Naushad M, Prakashmaran J, Gayathri V, Duaij O (2018) Phytoremediation of heavy metals: mechanisms, methods and enhancements. Environ Chem Lett 16:1339–1359. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10311-018-0762-3
                  
                ")]. Among them, phytoextraction has established great scope for application due to the low initial investment and opportunity of environmental problems related to metal-contaminated lands. Phytoextraction is the mechanism that plants absorb the contaminants from contaminated sites and accumulated in shoots, leaves and other plant parts [[42](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR42 "Rashid A, Mahmood T, Mehmood F, Khalid A, Saba B, Batool A, Riaz A (2014) Phytoaccumulation, competitive adsorption and evaluation of chelators-metal interaction in Lettuce plant. Environ Eng Manag J 13(10):2583–2592. 
                  https://doi.org/10.30638/eemj.2014.288
                  
                ")]. However, the remediation process is usually slow with single phytoextraction technique due to low plant biomass and low Cd accumulation efficiency [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR19 "Gavrilescu M (2022) Enhancing phytoremediation of soils polluted with heavy metals. Curr Opin Biotechnol 74:21–31. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copbio.2021.10.024
                  
                ")]. Therefore, it is of vital importance to improve the mobility of Cd and enhance the ability of hyperaccumulators to uptake and accumulate Cd in contaminated soil.

Recently, the combination of electrochemical remediation and phytoextraction has been attracted extensive attention because it can overcome the current weaknesses of the individual technologies [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR12 "Dey P, Osborne J, Lincy K (2024) An insight on the plausible biological and non-biological detoxification of heavy metals in tannery waste: a comprehensive review. Environ Res 258:119451. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2024.119451
                  
                ")]. The low-voltage electric field of the electrochemical-phytoremediation system can not only improve the bioavailability of heavy metals in soil by increasing metals desorption and transportation, but also stimulate plant growth and strengthen the accumulation of heavy metals in plants [[55](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR55 "Zhang X, Yang J, Qi L, Zhou W, Zhu Y, Li Z, Chen F, Guan C (2024) Evaluation of electrokinetic-assisted phytoremediation efficiency of dibutyl phthalate contaminated soil by maize (Zea mays L.) under different electric field intensities. Sci Total Environ 944:173838. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2024.173838
                  
                ")]. Previous studies have utilized direct electric current (DC) to improve phytoextraction capabilities [[51](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR51 "Xiao W, Ye X, Xu H, Yao G, Wang J, Li D, Zhang Q, Hu J, Gao N (2017) Intensification of phytoremediation of Cd contaminated soil with direct current field and soil amendments in addition to hyperaccumulator Sedum alfredii. Acta Pedol Sin 54(4):927–937. 
                  https://doi.org/10.11766/trxb201612130539
                  
                ")]. However, the prolonged use of DC causes OH− and H+ ions to migrate toward the cathode and anode, leading to excessive soil acidity and alkalinity, which can hinder plant growth and reduce remediation efficiency [[38](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR38 "Mulati H, Mamat A, Ailijiang N, Jiang L, Li N, Hu Y, Su Y (2023) Electrokinetic-assisted phytoremediation of Pb-contaminated soil: influences of periodic polarity reversal direct current field. Sustainability 15(11):11. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su15118439
                  
                ")]. In contrast, alternating electric current (AC) drives the movement of electric charge by periodically changing its direction in soil, which can overcome the defects of DC and significantly improve phytoextraction efficiency [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR23 "Hu J, Liu B, Xu X, Mao Z, Ye Z, Liu D, Fang X (2025) Effects of soil moisture and an AC-electric field on the phytoremediation of the Cd-contaminated soil. Environ Technol 46(5):761–771. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/09593330.2024.2368687
                  
                ")]. For example, Bi et al. [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR3 "Bi R, Schlaak M, Siefert E, Lord R, Connolly H (2011) Influence of electrical fields (AC and DC) on phytoremediation of metal polluted soils with rapeseed (Brassica napus) and tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum). Chemosphere 83(3):318–326. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2010.12.052
                  
                ")] reported that AC application enhanced biomass and accumulation of Cd in rapeseed (*Brassica napus*). Similar results have been obtained in oats (*Avena sativa* L.) [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR9 "Chirakkara R, Reddy K, Cameselle C (2015) Electrokinetic amendment in phytoremediation of mixed contaminated soil. Electrochim Acta 181:179–191. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2015.01.025
                  
                ")] and blue gum (*Eucalyptus globulus*) [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR33 "Luo J, Cai L, Qi S, Wu J, Gu X (2018) Influence of direct and alternating current electric fields on efficiency promotion and leaching risk alleviation of chelator assisted phytoremediation. Ecotoxicol Environ 149:241–247. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoenv.2017.12.005
                  
                ")]. Recently, several studies by our group have found that 0.5 V·cm⁻1 AC significantly promoted Cd accumulation in willow (*Salix sp.*) and *Sedum alfredii* Hance [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR4 "Bu A, Yao G, Zhou C, Mao Z, Liu B, Ma J, Fang X, Liu D, Ye Z (2024) Effect of AC electric field on enhancing phytoremediation of Cd-contaminated soils in different pH soils. Sci Rep 14(1):18035. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-68671-6
                  
                "), [36](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR36 "Mao Z, Liu B, Fang X, Ma J, Liu D, Gao Z, Castillo M, Ye Z (2022) Effects of different fertilizer treatments on AC electric field–assisted phytoremediation efficiency of Cd-contaminated soil by willow and Sedum. J Soils Sediments 22(5):1460–1468. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11368-022-03148-5
                  
                ")]. A large number of studies have explored the effects of the type, intensity and exposure duration of electric field on the phytoextraction ability. However, the influence of electric field frequency on phytoextraction of Cd-contaminated soil remains unclear. Studies have reported that applying an electric field at a frequency of 16 Hz promoted the root growth of chickpea (*Cicer arietinum*) [[43](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR43 "Rochalska M (2008) The influence of low frequency magnetic field upon cultivable plant physiology. Nukleonika 53(1):17–20")], whereas a frequency of 60 Hz electric field showed a similar promoting effect on tomato (*Solanum lycopersicum*) root growth [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR10 "De Souza A, Sueiro L, García D, Porras E (2010) Extremely low frequency non-uniform magnetic fields improve tomato seed germination and early seedling growth. Seed Sci Technol 38(1):61–72. 
                  https://doi.org/10.15258/sst.2010.38.1.06
                  
                ")]. Given the influence of electric field frequency on plant growth, we assume that electric field frequency may also affect the accumulation of metals in plants. However, more evidence is needed to verify this hypothesis.

In addition to plant-related factors, the efficiency of the electrochemical-phytoextraction system is also strongly dependent on the bioavailability of metals in the soil. Among various metals, Cd is of particular concern due to its high mobility, weak adsorption to soil colloids, and strong bioavailability even at low concentrations [[54](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR54 "Yitagesu YH (2019) Impacts of cement dust deposition on heavy metal pollution in soil and barley crop grown around Abyssinia cement factory. Ethiopia Chem Mater Res 11(2):1. 
                  https://doi.org/10.7176/cmr/11-2-01
                  
                ")]. The release and movement of Cd in soil are strongly influenced by soil characteristics. Earlier studies mainly focused on the effects of electric field on plant growth and Cd transport to roots by altering soil physicochemical properties [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR23 "Hu J, Liu B, Xu X, Mao Z, Ye Z, Liu D, Fang X (2025) Effects of soil moisture and an AC-electric field on the phytoremediation of the Cd-contaminated soil. Environ Technol 46(5):761–771. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/09593330.2024.2368687
                  
                ")]. As an important organism in soil ecosystem, soil microorganisms play a key role in material cycling and energy flow in soil–plant system [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR1 "Aqeel M, Ran J, Hu W, Irshad M, Dong L, Akram M, Eldesoky G, Aljuwayid A, Chuah L, Deng J (2023) Plant-soil-microbe interactions in maintaining ecosystem stability and coordinated turnover under changing environmental conditions. Chemosphere 318:137924. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2023.137924
                  
                ")]. Studies involving the effect of electric field on microorganisms have reported that electric field could trigger not only positive effects, such as acceleration of metabolic activity and stimulation of resistance to pollutants, but also negative effects, such as disruption of cellular membrane, and some indefinable effects on modifications in the microbial communities [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR2 "Beretta G, Mastorgio A, Pedrali L, Saponaro S, Sezenna E (2019) The effects of electric, magnetic and electromagnetic fields on microorganisms in the perspective of bioremediation. Rev Environ Sci Biotechnol 18:29–75. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11157-018-09491-9
                  
                "), [27](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR27 "Li F, Li J, Tong M, Xi K, Guo S (2023) Effect of electric fields strength on soil factors and microorganisms during electro-bioremediation of benzo [a] pyrene-contaminated soil. Chemosphere 341:139845. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2023.139845
                  
                ")]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that soil microorganisms enhance phytoremediation efficiency by stimulating promoting plant growth and strengthening plant tolerance to Cd stress [[41](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR41 "Qin H, Wang Z, Sha W, Song S, Qin F, Zhang W (2024) Role of plant-growth-promoting rhizobacteria in plant machinery for soil heavy metal detoxification. Microorganisms 12(4):4. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/microorganisms12040700
                  
                "), [57](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR57 "Zhu X, Lin F, Sun J, Li X, Zhu G, Lu Y, Sun L, Wang H (2024) Effects of weak electric fields on the denitrification performance of pseudomonas stutzeri: insights into enzymes and metabolic pathways. Microorganisms 12(6):1218. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/microorganisms12061218
                  
                ")]. Additionally, the bioavailability and mobility of metals in soil under Cd stress are governed by the metabolic activities of microorganisms and the expression of related functional genes [[48](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR48 "Tian J, Du Y, Yu C, Liu W, Zou R, Zhao Y, Zhang T, Jiang Y, Tian Z (2025) The influences of heavy metals on soil microbial C, N, P cycling and heavy metal resistance under different fertilization regimes. Environ Pollut 370:125915. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envpol.2025.125915
                  
                "), [56](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR56 "Zhu S, Mao H, Yang X, Zhao W, Sheng L, Sun S, Du X (2025) Resilience mechanisms of rhizosphere microorganisms in lead-zinc tailings: Metagenomic insights into heavy metal resistance. Ecotoxicol Environ Saf 292:117956. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoenv.2025.117956
                  
                ")]. It has also been reported that Cd contamination negatively affects the structure and function of soil microbial communities [[47](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR47 "Sun H, Shao C, Jin Q, Li M, Zhang Z, Liang H, Lei H, Qian J, Zhang Y (2022) Effects of cadmium contamination on bacterial and fungal communities in Panax ginseng-growing soil. BMC Microbiol 22(1):77. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s12866-022-02488-z
                  
                ")]. However, little is known about how soil microbial community structure and function respond to AC electric field and how this response regulates the remediation efficiency of electrochemical-phytoextraction techniques. In view of the fact that soil microorganisms may play an important regulatory role in the electrochemical-phytoextraction system. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the potential microbial mechanisms by which AC electric field affects phytoremediation.

Considering that willow (*Salix spp*.) possesses significant advantages in Cd accumulation because of its diverse varieties, rapid growth, substantial biomass, and well-developed root systems [[24](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR24 "Jiang C, Wang Y, Chen Y, Wang S, Mu C, Shi X (2024) The Phytoremediation Potential of 14 Salix Clones Grown in Pb/Zn and Cu Mine Tailings. Forests 15(2):2. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/f15020257
                  
                ")], it was selected as the experimental material in this study. This study adopts phytoextraction, a phytoremediation approach in which metals are absorbed by plant roots and translocated to above-ground tissues for accumulation. Willow is widely recognized as an effective extractor of Cd, as this metal tends to accumulate in its aerial parts [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR39 "Muthusaravanan S, Sivarajasekar N, Vivek J, Paramasivan T, Naushad M, Prakashmaran J, Gayathri V, Duaij O (2018) Phytoremediation of heavy metals: mechanisms, methods and enhancements. Environ Chem Lett 16:1339–1359. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10311-018-0762-3
                  
                ")]. While most recent studies have focused on the strength of the AC electric field [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR4 "Bu A, Yao G, Zhou C, Mao Z, Liu B, Ma J, Fang X, Liu D, Ye Z (2024) Effect of AC electric field on enhancing phytoremediation of Cd-contaminated soils in different pH soils. Sci Rep 14(1):18035. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-68671-6
                  
                "), [23](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR23 "Hu J, Liu B, Xu X, Mao Z, Ye Z, Liu D, Fang X (2025) Effects of soil moisture and an AC-electric field on the phytoremediation of the Cd-contaminated soil. Environ Technol 46(5):761–771. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/09593330.2024.2368687
                  
                "), [36](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR36 "Mao Z, Liu B, Fang X, Ma J, Liu D, Gao Z, Castillo M, Ye Z (2022) Effects of different fertilizer treatments on AC electric field–assisted phytoremediation efficiency of Cd-contaminated soil by willow and Sedum. J Soils Sediments 22(5):1460–1468. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11368-022-03148-5
                  
                ")], studies on the effects of changes in AC electric field frequency on phytoremediation are still limited. The primary aims of this study were to: (1) examine the influences of AC electric field at varying frequencies on the growth and Cd accumulation of willow; (2) explore the response characteristics of soil chemical and biological properties to AC electric field with different frequencies; and (3) clarify the relationship between phytoremediation efficiency and soil and plant factors under the influence of different AC electric field frequencies.

## Materials and methods

### Experimental design

A total of 50 kg Cd-contaminated soil was collected from farmland in Fuyang, District, Hangzhou City, Zhejiang Province. Composite sampling was conducted at a depth of 0–20 cm using a shovel, and soil from multiple subsampling points was thoroughly mixed to obtain a representative sample. The soil was thoroughly homogenized air-dried, ground, and sieved through a 2 mm mesh for subsequent experiment. The basic physicochemical properties of the soil are provided in Table S1. According to the Dutch Soil Quality Guidelines [[44](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR44 "RIVM. 2000. Dutch Target and Intervention Values for Soil Remediation. National Institute for Public Health and the Environment (RIVM), The Netherlands.")], Cd was the sole source of metal contamination (Table S2).

Willow cuttings were purchased from Mingshi Horticulture Company in Suqian City, Jiangsu Province. Willow branches were cut into approximately 15 cm pieces and used as experimental materials. A pot experiment was adopted and three treatments were set up. I, a control without AC (CK); II, 0.5 V·cm−1 AC at frequency of 50 Hz; III, 0.5 V·cm−1 AC at frequency of 300 Hz. Each treatment was replicated three times (n = 3). Each pot (20 cm in diameter and 15 cm in height) was filled with 4 kg of homogenized soil with 70% field water holding capacity. Except for the control, two graphite rods (1 cm in diameter and 15 cm in height) were placed at opposite ends of each pot. Each pot was transplanted with two willow cuttings, placed approximately 5 cm apart. The AC electric field was applied one week after cutting transplanting. The AC electric field was set to an intensity of 0.5 V·cm−1 at a frequency of 50 Hz or 300 Hz and readjusted weekly using a voltage regulator (TDGC2-0.5 kVA). The soil field capacity was maintained by weighing the pots every 2 days. The experimental treatment period was 2 months, after which plant and soil samples were collected for subsequent analyses.

### Sample analysis

#### SPAD values and root morphology parameters

SPAD values of mature willow leaves were measured using a SPAD-502 Plus chlorophyll meter (Japan). Root morphology was evaluated with an EPSON V700 scanner paired with Win RHIZO software, and the primary root lengths of the samples were documented.

#### Determination of N, P, K, and Cd contents in plant

After 2 months of treatment, the leaves, branches, stems, and roots of the willows were harvested and rinsed with deionized water. The samples were oven-dried at 105 °C for 15 min, followed by drying at 70 °C until reaching a constant weight, and were then ground into power for further analysis. Harvested willow tissues were digested with H2SO4–H2O2 to obtain a clear liquid (50 mL). The total N, P, and K contents in the plants were determined using the indophenol blue method, molybdenum–antimony colorimetry, and flame photometry, respectively. The Cd content of the plant tissues was analysed using graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS; SHIMADZU AA-7000, Kyoto, Japan) [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR23 "Hu J, Liu B, Xu X, Mao Z, Ye Z, Liu D, Fang X (2025) Effects of soil moisture and an AC-electric field on the phytoremediation of the Cd-contaminated soil. Environ Technol 46(5):761–771. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/09593330.2024.2368687
                  
                ")]. Cd accumulation in various tissues was quantified by calculating the product of the Cd concentration and the volume of the digested liquid in each organ.

#### Basic properties of soil

After treatment for 2 months, soil samples were collected, thoroughly mixed and air-dried, and passed through 10-mesh and 100-mesh sieves for subsequent analysis. The soil pH was determined using a pH meter (Sartorius, Göttingen, Germany). The contents of soil alkaline hydrolysable N, available P, and available K contents were analysed using the alkali N-proliferation method, the Olsen method, and flame photometry, respectively. The soil organic matter content was measured using the potassium dichromate external heating method.

#### Determination of soil Cd

The diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid extractable (DTPA) method was applied to extract available Cd from the soil. The total Cd content in the soil was extracted using the aqua regia dissolution method [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR23 "Hu J, Liu B, Xu X, Mao Z, Ye Z, Liu D, Fang X (2025) Effects of soil moisture and an AC-electric field on the phytoremediation of the Cd-contaminated soil. Environ Technol 46(5):761–771. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/09593330.2024.2368687
                  
                ")]. The DTPA-extracted solution and the digestion liquid were diluted with ultrapure water, and Cd concentrations were measured using GFAAS.

#### Assay of soil enzymes

To determine soil urease activity, a soil sample of 5 g was weighed and placed in a test tube, and mixed with 0.5 mL toluene, 20 mL citrate buffer (pH 6.7) and 10 mL urea solution (10%), followed by incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. The released ammonium was quantified calorimetrically using the indophenol blue method at a wavelength of 578 nm. A reaction solution without urea was used as the reference for each sample [[6](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR6 "Cang L, Zhou D, Wang Q, Wu D (2009) Effects of electrokinetic treatment of a heavy metal contaminated soil on soil enzyme activities. J Hazard Mater 172(2–3):1602–1607. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2009.08.033
                  
                ")].

For soil acidic phosphatase activity, a soil sample weighing 5 g was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h with 20 mL of acetate buffer (pH 5.0) containing 0.5% disodium phenyl phosphate. The phenol produced was extracted and oxidized with potassium hexacyanoferrate in an alkaline solution. The oxidation product was quantified using the 4-aminoantipyrine colorimetric method at a wavelength of 510 nm. An assay without soil or disodium phenyl phosphate was performed as a control [[6](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR6 "Cang L, Zhou D, Wang Q, Wu D (2009) Effects of electrokinetic treatment of a heavy metal contaminated soil on soil enzyme activities. J Hazard Mater 172(2–3):1602–1607. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2009.08.033
                  
                ")].

Catalase activity was measured as previously described [[21](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01177-8#ref-CR21 "Guan SY (1986) Soil Enzymes and Research Methods. China Agricultural Science Press, Beijing, China")]. A total of 2.5 mL H₂O₂ (0.3%) and 20 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) were added to the test tube, followed by incubation at 25 °C for 20 min. Subsequently, 2.5 mL of H₂SO₄ (3 mol L⁻1) was introduced to terminate the reaction, and the mixture was filtered using filter paper. The filtrate (15 mL) was titrated with 0.02 mol L⁻1 KMnO₄ solution, and the volume of KMnO₄ consumed was used to calculate CAT activity. Enzyme activity measurements were conducted in duplicate.

#### Soil microbiome analysis

The fresh soil (1 g) was taken, and microbial DNA was isolated from samples using the E.Z.N.A.® Soil. DNA Kit (Omega Bio-Tek, Norcross, GA, U.S.) following the provided protocol. The V1–V9 region of the 16S rRNA gene from bacteria was amplified by PCR under the following conditions: an initial denaturation at 95 °C for 2 min, followed by 27 cycles of 95 °C for 30 s, 55 °C for 30 s, and 72 °C for 60 s, with a final elongation at 72 °C for 5 min. The primers used were 27F (5′-AGRRTTYGATYMTGGCTCAG-3′) and 1492R (5′-RGYTACCTTGTTACGACTT-3′), each assigned a unique eight-base barcode to distinguish samples. PCR reactions were performed in triplicate with a 20-μL reaction mixture consisting of 4 μL 5 × FastPfu Buffer, 2 μL 2.5 mM dNTPs, 0.8 μL of each primer (5 μM), 0.4 μL FastPfu Polymerase, and 10 ng of template DNA. Amplicons were separated on 2% agarose gels and subsequently purified using an AxyPrep DNA Gel Extraction Kit (Axygen Biosciences, Union City, CA, U.S.).

SMRTbell libraries were constructed from the amplified DNA through blunt ligation, following the protocol provided by the manufacturer (Pacific Biosciences, Menlo Park, CA, U.S.). The purified SMRTbell libraries derived from the Zymo and HMP mock communities were sequenced using PacBio Sequel II 8 M cells using Sequencing Kit 2.0. Purified SMRTbell libraries from pooled and barcoded samples were sequenced on a single PacBio Sequel II cell. Amplicon sequencing was performed by Shanghai Biozero Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Raw reads generated by PacBio sequencing were analysed using SMRT Link Analysis software version 9.0 to produce demultiplexed circular consensus sequence (CCS) reads the settings included a minimum of 3 passes and a predicted accuracy threshold of 0.99. Further processing of raw reads was performed using SMRT Portal, which filtered sequences based on length (< 800 bp or > 2500 bp) and quality. Sequences failing to meet these criteria were trimmed accordingly.

Highlights
----------

*   •[Phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about Phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an economical and sustainable technology for environmental cleanup. 
*   •Plant species known to utilize different phyto-mechanisms during phytoremediation of diverse pollutants has been presented. 
*   •Different aids to overcome the limitations of phytoremediation are described. 
*   •Strengths and limitations inherent with the phytoremediation technique is supported by recent research. 

Abstract
--------

With current intensive agriculture practices and industrialization, pollution of natural resources like land and water with heavy metals, organic pollutants, radionuclides, pesticides, and fertilizers has become a major concern. Phytoremediation is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly technique that utilizes plants to immobilize, uptake, reduce toxicity, stabilize, or degrade the compounds that are released into the environment from different sources. Studies have shown that heavy metals, organic contaminants, radionuclides, antibiotics, and pesticides can be remediated using plants. Though phytoremediation has been practiced since decades, it is still an emerging technology. This review article summarizes existing information and synthesizes the recent findings on plant species suitable for use in phytoremediation through utilizing different mechanisms, aids that can enhance the efficiency of phytoremediation processes, and strengths and limitations that comes with the application of this technique. Diverse plants remediate different pollutants at different rates through one or multiple mechanisms. The limitations of phytoremediation can be overcome by using several aids including natural and chemical amendments, genetic engineering and natural microbial stimulation. Given the low-cost of phytoremediation compared to conventional technology and sustainability associated with plants and use of renewable energy, phytoremediation can be a reliable solution for a sustainable and economical remediation of soil and water from the organic and inorganic pollutants.

1. Introduction
---------------

Rapid rise in global population accompanied by ensuing demands for food, water, energy, timber, and other ecosystem services has led to severe stresses on the [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the environment ([Kamran et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0141); [Saleem et al., 2020c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0235); [Zaheer et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0298)). [Anthropogenic activities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/anthropogenic-activity "Learn more about Anthropogenic activities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including the agricultural intensification practices ([Mózner et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0192)), speedy urbanization, and rapid industrialization ([Cherniwchan,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0049); [Wu et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0289)) are causing serious environmental pollution by [heavy metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/heavy-metal "Learn more about heavy metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HMs) and metalloids ([Anyakora et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0015); [He et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0118); [Su,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0261)), radionuclides ([Hu et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0125); [Prakash et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0215)), organic compounds ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), agrochemicals ([Malik et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0177); [Sunitha et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0265)), and oil spills ([Ron & Rosenberg,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0228)). Mining activities, disposal of effluents from industries and [residential areas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/residential-area "Learn more about residential areas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), heavy use of fertilizers and pesticides, and irrigation of contaminated water have led to contamination of soils ([Awad et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0020); [Tang et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0267); [Teng et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0270)). Mining leads to disturbance of many soil properties, including pH, electrical conductivity, and cation exchange capacity ([Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Consequently, HMs such as Copper (Cu), Lead (Pb), Zinc (Zn), Nickel (Ni), Cadmium (Cd), Chromium (Cr), Iron (Fe), Manganese (Mn) and organic pollutants such as hydrocarbons, aromatic compounds, phenols, organo-chlorinated compounds, pesticides have been found at elevated concentrations in the soil and sediments ([Gutiérrez-Ginés et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0111); [Liu et al., 2017b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0169); [Song et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0254)).

HMs accumulation in soil affects [soil microbial population](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-microbial-populations "Learn more about soil microbial population from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), processes and their activity ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). The effects of HMs on microbial population changes and enzymatic activities are dependent upon the type of HMs and their chemical affinity ([Karaca et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0142)). For examples, cadmium (Cd) affects [ureases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/urease "Learn more about ureases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), proteases and basic phosphatases ([Lorenz et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0170)); lead (Pb) affects [catalases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/catalase "Learn more about catalases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ureases, [invertase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/invertase "Learn more about invertase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [acid phosphatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/acid-phosphatase "Learn more about acid phosphatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Belyaeva et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0032)); Arsenic (As) affects [sulfatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sulfatase "Learn more about sulfatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and phosphatase ([Speir et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0258)); and copper (Cu) restricts b-glucosidase ([Geiger et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0097)). High chromium (VI) concentration in soil exhibits distinct toxicity characteristics causing negative effects in soil microbial cell metabolism ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126)).

The [ingestion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ingestion "Learn more about ingestion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of food crops contaminated with HMs can lead to [bioaccumulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioaccumulation "Learn more about bioaccumulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of HMs and subsequent biomagnification along the higher [trophic levels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/trophic-level "Learn more about trophic levels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the food chain can threaten both animal and human health ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). HMs accumulation in human body can cause serious complications in human health such as Itai-itai disease due to chronic Cd exposure ([Pan et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0205)); kidney dysfunction and anemia due to Zn ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); [mucosa](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mucous-membrane "Learn more about mucosa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") corrosion, hepatic system failure and [central nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/central-nervous-system "Learn more about central nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") damage due to Cu ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); [skin irritation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/skin-irritation "Learn more about skin irritation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nervous-system "Learn more about nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") complications due to Ni ([Argun et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0016)); cardiovascular problems and damage to nervous system due to Pb ([Kazemipour et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0144); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); abortion, nervous breakdown and skin rashes due to Hg ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); cancer due to Cr ([Zohdi et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0304)); and carcinogenic and [immune system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/immune-system "Learn more about immune system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") dysfunction due to As ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)).

Among different organic contaminants, chlorinated solvents, petroleum hydrocarbons explosives ([Alkorta & Garbisu,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0011)), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), pyrene, and trinitrotoluene (TNT) ([Chekol & Vough,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0045)) are major soil contaminants. Pesticides that often contaminate soils include [atrazine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/atrazine "Learn more about atrazine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [dieldrin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/dieldrin "Learn more about dieldrin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [hexachlorobenzene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/hexachlorobenzene "Learn more about hexachlorobenzene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), dichlorodiphenyltrichlorethane (DDT), and 1,2,4- trichlorobenzene ([Chiou et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0050)). These pollutants seriously affect the [soil biota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-biota "Learn more about soil biota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mineral nutrients availability, and soil productivity because of their persistence in soil for long period of time and toxicity.

Like soils, [water resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/water-resources "Learn more about water resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have also been contaminated by different pollutants. Urbanization and industrialization activities release toxic heavy metals through the sewage, runoff and effluents contributing to largest share of factors for water contamination ([Masindi & Muedi,2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0182); [Parveen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0206); [Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Over 80% of world's wastewater reach to environment without treatment causing nearly 58% of health issues like diarrhea, which is a major cause for early child dealth ([Connor et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0054)). Among metals, Cu, Ni, Pb, Cd, Cr, U, and Zn frequently contaminate water resources ([Prasad & Freitas,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0216)). The HMs degrade the quality of water and can lead to water-borne health hazards through heavy metal accumulation in the food chain ([Wongsasuluk et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0287)). Apart from HMs, organic pollutants contaminate water resources either by erosion, seepage, sludge and effluents from municipal waste and industries, and runoff from agricultural practices ([Diez,2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0064)) and mining ([Tanhan et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0268)). These contaminants can accumulate over time and negatively affect the aquatic as well as [terrestrial ecosystems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/terrestrial-ecosystem "Learn more about terrestrial ecosystems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Remediation of the soil and water pollutants for sustaining ecosystem processes and functions is one of the major challenges our society [faces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/face "Learn more about faces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") today. Many physical, chemical, and biological approaches have been employed for remediating the environmental pollution, however, their applications are limited due to the cost and labor requirements, safety hazards, and risks to ecosystems ([Ali et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0010)). An alternative technique, [phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), can be an effective technique and is gaining popularity, acceptance, and implementation.

Phytoremediation technique utilizes plants to remediate the contaminated media including soil and water. Phytoremediation is an economically and environmentally favorable technique as it utilizes [green plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/viridiplantae "Learn more about green plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to contain, sequester or detoxify contaminants from contaminated soil and water ([Ashraf et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0019)). Phytoremediation utilizes many mechanisms including degradation (rhizo-degradation, phytodegradation), accumulation (phytoextraction, rhizofiltration), dissipation (phytovolatilization), and immobilization (hydraulic control and phytostabilization) to degrade, remove, or immobilize the pollutants ([Pivetz,2001b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0214)). Depending upon the contaminants, plants utilize one or more of these mechanisms to reduce their concentrations from soil and water. For examples, plants uptake and accumulate the HMs in their tissues ([Mahar et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0176)) and degrade the organic pollutants ([Saleem et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0234)) reducing their toxicity from soil and water resources.

Different plant uses different methods or their combinations to remediate soil and water depending upon the contaminant types, forms, and media. Contaminated ground water can be remediated through phytodegradation, phytovolatilization, rhizo-filtration, rhizo-degradation and phytodegradation. Surface and wastewater contaminations can be treated by [rhizofiltration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizofiltration "Learn more about rhizofiltration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), phytodegradation or rhizodegradation. Soil, sediments or sludges contaminations are remediated through phytoextraction, phytodegradations, phytostabilization, rhizodegradation or phytovolatilization.

Ideal plant species for phytoremediation purpose should be hardy in nature, high biomass-producing, tolerant to toxic effects of metals and contaminants, easy to cultivate, have high absorption capacity, and be non-attractive for herbivory ([Adesodun et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0002); [Sakakibara et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0231); [Shabani & Sayadi,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0243)). In many cases, plant species cannot perform well and need aids to enhance phytoremediation. Such aids include soil amendments like biochar ([Paz-Ferreiro et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0207)), Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA) ([Shahid et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0244)), endophytic bacteria ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), [Arbuscular mycorrhiza](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arbuscular-mycorrhiza "Learn more about Arbuscular mycorrhiza from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Gaur & Adholeya,2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0095)) or even [transgenic plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/transgenic-plant "Learn more about transgenic plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Abhilash et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0001); [Kawahigashi,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0143)). They promote the phytoremediation process either by reducing the toxicity of pollutants or increasing the availability of pollutants or promoting the growth of plants.

Previous articles have provided information regarding techniques ([Jadia & Fulekar,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0132); [Sharma & Pathak,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0245); [Willey,2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0285)), plants used ([Banuelos et al., 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0027); [Cheng,2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0047); [Pilon-Smits & Pilon,2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0211)), factors affecting the processes ([Antoniadis et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0014); [Sheoran et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0246)), aids or stimulators ([Coninx et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0053); [Hovsepyan & Greipsson,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0123); [Ullah et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0273)) and are either specific to soil ([Belouchrani et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0031); [Romeh,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0227); [Waigi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0278)) or water resources ([Nazir et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0197); [Rezania et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0226); [Ugya & Imam,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0272)). enriched_points.expanded_content: Field trials demonstrated various phytoremediation mechanisms, including phytoextraction and phytostabilization, showcasing their capacity to absorb and stabilize contaminants effectively.
Significant reductions in heavy metals like Pb, Hg, and Cd were observed, with plants such as Amaranthus retroflexus showing the highest accumulation rates in their tissues.
Phytoremediation practices led to increased plant diversity, promoting a richer ecosystem that supports various organisms and enhances overall soil health.
Trials indicated improved soil structure and nutrient availability, with enhanced microbial activity contributing to overall soil health and fertility.
Successful phytoremediation projects provided local communities with cleaner environments, increased awareness of sustainable practices, and potential economic benefits through ecosystem services.
